content.full_text:
Fern PoLrrrcAL PnacrrcEs CovrrrarssroN
428 J Street. Suite 620. Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660. Fax (916) 322-0886
March 28,2003
Ellen Corey Born, Interim Port Attorney
Port of San Diego
Post Office Box 120488
San Diego, CA 92112-0488
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our FiIe No. A-03-025
Dear Ms. Born:
This leffer is in response to your request for advice on behalf of port
Commissioner Peter Q. Davis regarding the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
ttAct").1
QUESTION
Are you required to report either the sale of "calls," or "calls" that are repurchased
from the buyer or "calls" that expire?
CONCLUSION
No. Individual transactions thernselves need not be disclosed on your Statement
of Economic Interests. The investments themselves, such as shares, in an institution are
reported on Schedule A-1 or A-2 of the Form 700, whereas income from that institution
is reported on Schedule C.
FACTS
The San Diego Unified Port District ("District") is governed by the San Diego
Board of Port Commissioners ("Board") and has jurisdiction over tidelands surrounding
San Diego Bay, submerged lands in the bay, and any other lands acquired by the District,
which are held in trust for the benefit of the people of the State of California for the
purposes of commerce, navigation, fisheries and recreation, as codified in the Act.
I Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
-!:


File No. 4-03-025
Page No. 2
The purpose of your request for written advice is to ascertain whether the selling
of "calls," or the resulting scenarios arising from the sale of "calls" must be reported on a
Statement of Economic Interests, Form 700 due to Mr. Davis's position as a port
commissioner and in view of the facts.
On August 6,2002, Mr. Davis was appointed to the position of port commissioner
at the District. A port commissioner is a "full disclosure" position, as that term is used
when identifting reiorting requirements pursuant to the potiticA Reform Act. Through a
brokerage firm ("Broker"), wholly owned by a commercial financial institution
("institution"), Mr. Davis sells "calls" on shares of the instifution representing well over
one million dollars in face value securities. There are several scenarios that can occur
once "calls" are sold. For instancg "calls" are sold at a fixed amount and then: a)
repurchased from the seller; b) the "call" is exercised by the buyer; or c) the ,'call"
expires. Mr. Davis's income from this activity is well above one hundred thousand
dollars, but below one million dollars. He reported his ownership ofinstitution shares on
Schedule A-1, Form 700, as his ownership interest in the institution is less than ten
percent. Additionally, he reports his income from the institution on Schedule C, Form
700.
ANALYSIS
The Act requires all public officials designated in their agency's conflict of
interest code to file a Form 700, Statement of Economic Interests ("SEI"), each year
disclosing investrrents, real property interests, income and gifts that they have received
during the year. ($ 87300 et seq.) As a commissioner of the San Diego Port Authority,
you must disclose this information on your Statement of Economic Interests. You must
disclose, among other things, your investnents, as defined by the Act.
($ 87206.) Commissioners first report the invesfinents held as of the date they assume
office. ($ 87302.)
The Act defines "investrnent" to include "any financial interest in or security
issued by a business entity, including but not limited to common stock, preferred stock,
rights, warrants, options, debt instruments and any partnership or other ownership
interest...." ($ 82034.)
You indicated that you already reported your ownership of institution shares on
Schedule A-1, Form 700, as your interest in the instifution is less than ten percent. Also,
you reported your income from the institution on Schedule C, Form 700. You ask,
however, whether the selling of "calls" on shares of the institution requires additional
reporting of those transactions beyond that which you already reported.
You indicated in a phone message left with this agency that the "calls" are in the
same nature as "options" in a stock. Generally, stock "options" allow the holder of the
option to "exercise" the option by buying the stock at a future time at an agreed upon
price. For instance, one may have an option to buy 100 shares of institution stocli in
January of 2004 at apice of $10 per share. In January of 2O04,the holder of the stock


File No. A-03-025
PageNo.3
option may "exercise" the option and purchase the shares at $10 - even if the stock
actually is trading at a higher value atthattime. In the scenario you pose, you hold
shares of the institution and sell the option to buy those shares to others at a future date.
As long as you report ownership of those shares of the institution, you have complied
with this requirement of the Act.
Whether you must report the purchaser of the "calls" is a different question. If a
public ofiEcial sells his or her stock on the open market and does not know the identity of
the buyer, the public official will no longer have an economic interest in that business
entity nor would he or she have an economic interest in the buyer. ($ 82030, subd.
OX12).) You indicated on your message that you sell the "calls" through a broker who
handles these transactions for you. If so, and the above rule is true in your case, then you
need not disclose the purchaser of the call as a source of income to you.
On the other hand, if an official sells his or her stock privately and knows the
identity of the buyer, the official will have to report the buyer as a source of income on
his Statement of Economic Interests. ($ 82030, subd.(a); Crabb Advice Letter, A-00-
066.)
On the annual Statement of Economic Interests, public officials disclose
investments, business positions and real property held, and income received, in the prior
calendar year. However, because you were appointed to yourposition as port
commissioner on August 6,2002, the period covered by your annual statement will be
August 7, 2002, through December 31,2002. (You should already have filed an
assuming office statement within 30 days of your appointment.) You must report
investnent interests held and income received during that period even if you no longer
hold those investment interests or the source of income no longer is a source.of income to
you.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa
General
C. Scott Tocher
Counsel, Legal Division
CSTjg
I:\Adviceltrs\03-025
By
